title: Household Survey Shows Modest Retreat in Reported and Expected Spending Growth

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: The New York Fed's December 2022 Survey of Consumer Expectations Household Spending Survey shows a decline in reported monthly household spending growth to 7.7% year-over-year from a series high of 9.0% in August 2022, while year-ahead expected spending growth retreated to 4.0%, the lowest reading since April 2021 but still well above pre-pandemic levels. The share of households making large purchases fell to 56.4% from 61.7% in August, with vehicle purchases declining to their lowest level since August 2020.
key_points: Median reported year-over-year household spending growth declined to 7.7% in December 2022 from 9.0% in August 2022, but remains well above the pre-pandemic level of 2.5% in December 2019
Year-ahead expected spending growth fell to 4.0% in December, down from 4.4% in August, the lowest reading since April 2021
56.4% of households reported making at least one large purchase in the last four months, down from 61.7% in August
Vehicle purchases fell to the lowest level since August 2020 and remain below pre-pandemic levels

In response to an unexpected 10% income increase, households would allocate 33.8% to debt paydown (up from 31.4% in December 2021), 47.7% to saving/investing (down from 48.8%), and 18.5% to spending/donating (down from 19.7%)
Expected spending growth declined most for households with income over $100,000
why_it_matters: The content is accurate but not relevant to 'market structure' target. If reclassified as macro data, the why_it_matters should note this is consumer spending/macro data rather than market structure content.
macro_context: This survey data contributes to understanding consumer spending trends and inflation dynamics. The decline in both reported and expected spending growth suggests households are adjusting behavior in response to high inflation and economic uncertainty, which has implications for Fed policy path considerations.
important_numbers: 7.7% — median reported year-over-year spending growth, December 2022
56.4% — share of households making large purchases
33.8% — share of unexpected income increase that would go to debt paydown
47.7% — share of unexpected income increase that would be saved or invested
18.5% — share of unexpected income increase that would be spent or donated
